content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 1, 2007

Heather Irwin
Assistant General Counsel
State Bar of California
180 Howard Street
San Francisco, California 94105-1639

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-060

Dear Ms. Irwin:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the lobbyist provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Is the State Bar of California (the "State Bar") a lobbyist employer subject to registration under the Act?

CONCLUSION

No. Because the State Bar's lobbyist is an employee who is designated in its conflict of interest code, he falls under the exemption in Section 86300, and the State Bar does not have reporting obligations.

FACTS

You are an attorney with the State Bar's Office of General Counsel. The State Bar is a public corporation established in the judicial branch of government for the purpose of performing governmental functions in the administration of justice. (Cal. Const., art. VI, § 9; Bus. & Prof. Code § 6001.) In matters of admission and discipline of attorneys, the State Bar acts as an administrative arm of the California Supreme Court and is subject to the immediate and plenary review of the high court. In 1985, the State Bar received an advisory letter from the General Counsel of the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") stating that, because the State Bar is a "state agency" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, both the State Bar and its in-house lobbyist are exempt from reporting and registration obligations that Chapter 6 of the Act imposes.

_______________
1 Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-07-060
Page No. 2

In 1997, the Commission clarified the State Bar's reporting requirements in the
Goldman Advice letter, I-97-532, stating that the exclusion for employees of the State of
California sometimes applies to consultants or outside counsel hired by a state agency.
The letter also stated that a consultant meets the definition of an "employee the state of
California" for purposes of Section 86300 if the consultant is covered by the state
agency's conflict of interest code. In 1997, the State Bar qualified as a lobbyist
employer.

You have written to us again as the State Bar has recently changed its practices.
Currently, the State Bar does not use any outside lobbyists. In February of this year,
Anthony Williams, the Director of Governmental Affairs, became a part-time employee
with the State Bar. In his non-State Bar time, he engages in lobbying for other entities.
He has registered as a lobbyist and as part of a lobbying firm.

ANALYSIS

Under the Act, a lobbyist employer has specified accounting and reporting
obligations. (Sections 86116, 86116.5; Regulations 18615, 18616.) A lobbyist employer
includes any person, other than a lobbying firm, who employs one or more lobbyists for
the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action. (Section 82039.5.) The
Act, however, exempts from the lobbyist provisions of the Act "employees of the State of
California acting within the scope of [their] employment." (Section 86300(a).)
Lobbyists who work for state agencies are still subject to the ten dollar gift limitation,
however. (Ibid.)

As stated above, for the purposes of the Act, this office considers the State Bar a
"state agency." Therefore, if the State Bar employs a lobbyist who is designated in the
State Bar's conflicts of interest code, he or she is exempt from the lobbyist provisions of
the Act. As we have previously advised, if an employee subject to the exemption in
Section 86300 is already a registered lobbyist, he or she is not required to report
payments received from a state agency for lobbying services. (See Quessenberry Advice
Letter, A-84-121 (copy enclosed).) For these reasons, at this time the State Bar does not
have reporting obligations under the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

ADVICE FILE SUMMARY SHEET

Requestor Name | Heather Irwin, Office of General Counsel
Advisee Name | State Bar of California/Anthony Williams
Agency Name | State Bar of California
Letter Number | AI-07-060 | Date Issued | 05/01/07

SUBJECT OF LETTER

C/I | X | C/I Code | Campaign | Honoraria | 84308
Campaign | | Lobbying | | Gift Limits
SEI | | Rev. Door | |
Co-Sponsored | | Personal Use | | Mass Mailing
Prop. 34 | | Misc | |

BULLETIN SUMMARY

The State Bar of California requested information regarding its updated lobbying practices. Staff advised that where the State Bar no longer engages an independent contractor lobbyist, but instead a part-time employee who is covered in the conflict of interests code, the State Bar is not a lobbyist employer.

Notes/Superseded Letters:

OK          D/I          4/30/07

APR-03-2007 15:21     STATE BAR GENERAL COUNSEL     415 539 2321    P.02

THE STATE BAR
OF CALIFORNIA

180 HOWARD STREET, SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94105-1639

097-060

OFFICE OF GENERAL COUNSEL

TELEPHONE: (416) 638-2339 / FAX: (416) 638-2321

April 9, 2007

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 620
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE:     Advice on Filing Requirements

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am an attorney in the Office of General Counsel of the State Bar of California. I am
requesting advice on our ongoing filing requirements for our in-house lobbyists. We
have received two advice letters on the subject in the past – the Rosenthal Advice letter
(No. A-85-032) and the Goldman Advice letter (No. I-97-532).

As noted in the two letters, the State Bar is a public corporation established in the judicial
branch of government for the purpose of performing governmental functions in the
administration of justice. (Cal. Const., art. VI, § 9; Bus. & Prof. Code § 6001.) In matters
of admission and discipline of attorneys, the State Bar acts as an administrative arm of
the California Supreme Court and is subject to the immediate and plenary review of the
high court. In the 1985 Rosenthal advice letter, the General Counsel of the Fair Political
Practices Commission advised that because the State Bar is a "state agency" within the
meaning of the Political Reform Act, both the State Bar and its in-house lobbyists are
exempt from reporting and registration obligations imposed by Chapter 6 of the Act.

The Goldman Advice letter clarified the State Bar's reporting requirements as a "state
agency" with regard to independent contractor lobbyists. The Goldman letter stated that
the exclusion for employees of the State of California sometimes applies to consultants or
outside counsel hired by a state agency. It noted that generally a consultant meets the
definition of an "employee of the State of California" as used in section 86300 if the
consultant is covered by the state agency's conflict of interest code.

In 1997, the State Bar's lobbyist did not fall within the exemption of 86300. At that time,
the State Bar had a contract with Mel Assagai, an independent contractor lobbyist who
was not included in the Bar's conflict of interest code. Accordingly, the Bar qualified as a
employer and had to file quarterly disclosure reports.

APR-09-2007 15:21     STATE BAR GENERAL COUNSEL          415 538 2321   P.03

     The State Bar currently does not use any outside lobbyists. On February 12, 2007, the
Director of Governmental Affairs, Anthony Williams, became a part-time employee with
the State Bar, rather than a full time employee. Since he is on a reduced schedule, he is
engaging in lobbying activities for other entities in his non-State Bar time. In order to do
this, he has registered as a lobbyist and as part of a lobbying firm.

     The Billy Advice (No. I-02-204) letter stated that "[u]nder the first test of section
820383, a business entity will be a lobbying firm if any of its partners, owners, officers
or employees is a lobbyist and the business entity receives compensation for the lobbying
services it provides on behalf of others." Vernon Bill was a registered lobbyist and was
doing work for two entities. The FPPC advised that therefore Mr. Billy no longer
qualified as an in-house lobbyist, but would be considered an individual contract lobbyist
for both the firm and the school district.

     Under the reasoning of the Billy Advice letter, it appears that Mr. Williams is now an
"individual contract lobbyist." However, he is still an employee of the State Bar in all
other respects, rather than an independent contractor. As such, he is still designated in
our conflict of interest code.¹ Under the Rosenthal and Goldman Advice letters, he should
be considered as an "employee of the State of California" as used in section 86300.
Therefore, it appears that the State Bar is currently exempt from the lobbyist employer
reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act. Please advise us whether you are in
agreement with this analysis.

     Thank you for your consideration. You can contact me at (415) 538-2029. I look
forward to hearing from you.


     Very truly yours,

     [signature]
     Heather Irwin
     Assistant General Counsel
     State Bar of California




¹ The State Bar also has a category in its Conflict of Interest Code for designated consultants.


                                                                    TOTAL P.03

QUESTION: Is the State Bar of California (the "state Bar') a lobbyist employer subject to
registration under the Act?

CONCLUSION: No' Because the State Bar's lobbyist is an ernployee who is designated in its
conflict of interest code, he falls under the exemption in Section 86300, and the State Bar
does not have reporting obligations.
